ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a jasmine tea class (FOODON_03301103); yuzu-jasmine tea is a flavoured variant not separately coded. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for yuzu jasmine tea. AN0KL (Tea, infusions and similar beverages) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 16313001 (Tea substance) is the closest parent; no specific code exists for yuzu jasmine tea.

preparation_method: chilled or served at room temperature

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is a 500 ml bottle or cup of yuzu jasmine tea, as sold by See Ya Tonight and similar Chinese-style beverage vendors in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Tea, ready-to-drink, jasmine, brewed; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods; supplemented with yuzu juice nutrient data from USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for yuzu jasmine tea exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA Branded Foods. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for brewed jasmine tea (FDC ID 171917) as the dominant base, with a small upward adjustment to sugar_g and energy_kcal to reflect typical vendor addition of yuzu syrup or sweetener (~5–8 g sugar per 100 ml in commercial preparations). Sodium is taken from the brewed tea baseline. All values are per 100 g (approximately 100 ml for this beverage).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; jasmine tea / sweetened tea analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for yuzu jasmine tea. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on lightly sweetened jasmine/green tea beverages reported in Atkinson et al. 2021 (Am J Clin Nutr). The GI of plain tea is effectively zero; the addition of ~5 g sugar per 100 ml raises the effective GI to approximately 45 (low range), consistent with lightly sweetened tea beverages in the Sydney GI database. Glycemic load per 500 ml serving = (45 × 25 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 11.25, rounded to 11.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~15 mg per 500 ml serving)
hypertension.guidance: Yuzu jasmine tea is very low in sodium (~15 mg per 500 ml) and poses minimal concern for hypertension; individuals should however monitor added-sugar versions as excess sugar intake can indirectly elevate blood pressure over time.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Free sugar content from added yuzu syrup or sweetener (~24 g per 500 ml serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Each 500 ml serving contains approximately 24–25 g of free sugars from added sweetener, which contributes meaningfully to postprandial glucose load; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request unsweetened or reduced-sugar versions and account for the glycemic load of approximately 11 per serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible fat and sodium; jasmine tea polyphenols may confer mild cardioprotective benefit
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Yuzu jasmine tea contains no fat or cholesterol and very little sodium, making it a cardiovascular-friendly beverage choice; jasmine green tea polyphenols (catechins) have been associated with modest reductions in LDL cholesterol in observational studies, though the clinical magnitude depends on polyphenol concentration in the commercial preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Yuzu jasmine tea (柚子茉莉花茶) is a lightly sweetened floral tea beverage popular in Chinese-style tea shops and beverage stalls across Singapore. It is prepared by brewing jasmine green tea — itself made by scenting green tea leaves with fresh jasmine blossoms — and blending it with yuzu juice, yuzu syrup, or yuzu extract. Yuzu (柚子, Citrus junos) is a tart East Asian citrus fruit with a distinctive floral-citrus aroma that bridges lemon, grapefruit, and mandarin. The resulting drink has a delicate floral fragrance from the jasmine, a bright citrus tartness from the yuzu, and a light sweetness from added syrup. It is typically served chilled or over ice in a 500 ml cup or bottle. Nutritionally, a 500 ml serving provides approximately 100 kcal, predominantly from added sugars (~24–25 g), with negligible protein and fat. The beverage is naturally low in sodium and contains small amounts of vitamin C from the yuzu component. Jasmine green tea contributes polyphenols including catechins, which have been studied for antioxidant and mild cardioprotective properties. The glycemic load per 500 ml serving is approximately 11 (low-to-moderate), making it a better choice than sugar-sweetened sodas but still a source of free sugars that individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should moderate. It is suitable for vegetarian, vegan, halal, and gluten-free diets. In Singapore, this drink is commonly sold at Chinese beverage chains and hawker stalls as a refreshing alternative to plain tea or fruit juice.
common_user_queries: How many calories in yuzu jasmine tea?
Is yuzu jasmine tea good for diabetics?
What is 柚子茉莉花茶?
Is yuzu jasmine tea high in sugar?
Does yuzu jasmine tea have caffeine?
What is the GI of yuzu jasmine tea?
Is yuzu jasmine tea suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for yuzu jasmine tea exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or any primary regional nutrition database. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central brewed jasmine tea data with a reasonable upward adjustment for typical commercial sweetener addition. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature on lightly sweetened tea beverages. Confidence is medium rather than low because the estimation methodology is transparent, the base food is well-characterised, and the adjustments are conservative and documented.
notes: 1. protein_g is naturally zero: yuzu jasmine tea is a water-based infused beverage with no protein-containing ingredients in meaningful quantity. Confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central brewed jasmine tea baseline (FDC ID 171917). 2. fat_g is naturally zero: no fat-containing ingredients are present in this beverage. Confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 3. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg are all naturally zero for the same reason. 4. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as the beverage is a liquid infusion with no intact plant fibre. 5. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central jasmine tea entry and is not a significant constituent of this beverage. 6. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for brewed jasmine tea. 7. vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database. 8. vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database. 9. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database. 10. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database; tea contains no B12. 11. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the source database. 12. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database; tea contains no vitamin D. 13. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database. 14. phosphorus_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database. 15. magnesium_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database. 16. zinc_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database. 17. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database. 18. GI set to 45 (low category) as an analogous estimate for a lightly sweetened jasmine tea; plain tea GI is effectively zero but the added sugar component raises the effective GI. GL per 500 ml serving = (45 × 25) / 100 = 11.25 ≈ 11.3. 19. This record is served by 1 restaurant (See Ya Tonight 今晚有局, Singapore Level 1) on field survey data at SGD 5.20. 20. Multilingual names follow Singaporean dish rules (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as this is a Chinese-cuisine beverage sold in Singapore.

improvement_actions_taken: Added zero-macro justification phrases in notes for protein_g, fat_g, saturated_fat_g, and dietary_fibre_g (naturally zero).
Set glycemic_index to 45 (not 1) because this beverage contains ~5 g sugar per 100 ml from added sweetener, making it a genuinely low-GI sweetened beverage rather than a zero-carbohydrate drink.
Documented all -1 sentinel values in notes with explicit source-not-reported justifications.
Ensured all ontology match_type values use only the three allowed values: exact, closest_parent, none.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list are perfectly consistent — all strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in aliases.
Applied Singaporean dish multilingual rules: Chinese (simplified + traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi all populated.
Used human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' with machine key type2_diabetes.
